one_line_takeaway: An entropy-gated, semantics-driven, temperature-scaling watermark that keeps LLM text near-natural in perplexity and resists paraphrasing while cutting spoofing-attack decryption from ~0.96 (fixed green list) to 0.571, all with prompt- and model-agnostic detection.
key_finding: Under paraphrase attacks the method retains detectability better than logit-bias baselines while keeping near-un-watermarked perplexity and substantially better spoofing resistance. On OPT-6.7B with GPT-3.5 paraphrasing it reaches ROC-AUC 0.981 vs KGW-0 0.964, KGW-1 0.874, KGW-2 0.769, EXP-edit 0.881; under DIPPER 0.964 vs KGW-0 0.936. For security, its strengthened-spoofing decryption rate is only 0.571 vs KGW-0 0.963, KGW-1 0.912, KGW-2 0.723 (lower is better). Ablation shows AWTI both lowers perplexity (median >10 at alpha=0 falls sharply as alpha rises) and can improve robustness by filtering edited tokens (ROC-AUC 0.978 at alpha=0 vs 0.988 at alpha=1), while cutting the Average Watermark Rate to 0.553 at alpha=3. Unlike prior green-list methods, perplexity does not rise with watermark strength delta because AWTS scales temperature.
core_contribution: The paper proposes 'Adaptive Watermark,' a decoding-time watermarking scheme for LLM-generated text that jointly targets robustness, security, and text quality while remaining detectable in an 'agnostic' manner (without access to the original LLM or the prompt). Its own contribution has three modules: (1) Adaptive Watermark Token Identification (AWTI), which uses a small auxiliary Measurement Model (e.g., GPT-2 or OPT-350M) to estimate Shannon entropy per position and perturbs only high-entropy tokens (H >= alpha), leaving low-entropy tokens untouched; (2) Semantic-based Logits Scaling Vector Extraction (SLSVE), which derives a per-token binary logits-scaling vector from the semantics of previously generated text via a Sentence Embedding model (Sentence-BERT / all-mpnet-base-v2) feeding a trained Semantic Mapping Model, replacing the fixed secret-key green/red list; and (3) Adaptive Watermark Temperature Scaling (AWTS), which injects the watermark by multiplicatively scaling logits (l_hat = l * (1 + delta * v_hat)) rather than adding a fixed bias to green tokens. Detection re-runs AWTI to find candidate tokens, recovers the scaling vector via SLSVE, and scores via an approximate likelihood-ratio test that reduces to averaging delta * v_hat over candidate tokens.
threat_model: Two adversary goals are considered. (1) Robustness adversary: a user paraphrases/edits watermarked text to remove the watermark while preserving meaning; evaluated with GPT-3.5 (gpt-3.5-turbo-instruct) and DIPPER paraphrasing. (2) Security/forgery adversary (spoofing attack of Sadasivan et al. 2023): an attacker who can repeatedly query the watermarked model analyzes token-frequency statistics to reverse-engineer the green list and forge text that a detector would flag as AI-generated, damaging the system's reputation. The detector is assumed NOT to have the original prompt or LLM (agnostic detection). The paper notes its reported decryption rate is an upper bound because it strengthens the spoofing attack by fixing a single semantic embedding across all generations, whereas a real attacker faces varying semantics per generation.
limitations: Detection depends on the auxiliary Measurement Model and a secret opening sentence, and hyperparameters (alpha, delta) must be tuned per model (delta=1.5 for OPT-6.7B, delta=0.6 for Mistral-7B), so performance is model-specific and requires the owner to train a model-matched small measurement model. Security is evaluated only via one spoofing attack under a deliberately weakened (fixed-embedding) setting that the authors frame as an upper bound on decryption, not a proof of unforgeability; no adaptive attacker against SLSVE itself is studied. The likelihood-ratio detection score relies on approximations (softmax normalization ratio ~ 1 and dropping unknown original logits l_k). No formal/theoretical guarantee is provided (authors defer theoretical analysis to future work). Main-text tables report a single dataset (C4 realnewslike) and mostly OPT-6.7B for security/ablation; broader results are in appendices.

datasets_used: C4 realnewslike subset (Raffel et al. 2020) - first two sentences as prompt, next 200 tokens as human text
Sentence-Compression dataset - used to train the Semantic Mapping Model
quantitative_results: OPT-6.7B, GPT-3.5 paraphrase, ROC-AUC: Ours 0.981 vs KGW-0 0.964, KGW-1 0.874, KGW-2 0.769, EXP-edit 0.881
OPT-6.7B, DIPPER paraphrase, ROC-AUC: Ours 0.964 vs KGW-0 0.936, KGW-1 0.842, KGW-2 0.795, EXP-edit 0.883
OPT-2.7B, no attack, ROC-AUC: Ours 1.000 (Best F1 1.000)
OPT-2.7B, GPT-3.5 paraphrase, ROC-AUC: Ours 0.983 vs KGW-0 0.971 (best baseline)
Security decryption rate on OPT-6.7B (lower=better): Ours 0.571 vs KGW-0 0.963, KGW-1 0.912, KGW-2 0.723
Ablation (OPT-6.7B, GPT-3.5 paraphrase) entropy threshold ROC-AUC: alpha=0 0.978, alpha=1 0.988, alpha=2 0.981, alpha=3 0.972; Average Watermark Rate falls 1.000 -> 0.835 -> 0.707 -> 0.553
Ablation watermark strength (OPT-6.7B) ROC-AUC: delta=0.5 0.982, delta=1 0.984, delta=1.5 0.981, delta=2 0.978
Default hyperparameters alpha=2, M=50; delta=1.5 for OPT-6.7B, delta=0.6 for Mistral-7B; sampling Top-K=50, Top-p=0.9
Spoofing setup: 5,000 generated sentences, 181 common tokens, top-50 highest-frequency tokens examined against green list
baselines_compared: KGW-0 (Zhao et al. 2023) - fixed green/red list from shared key
KGW-1 / KGW-2 (Kirchenbauer et al. 2023a) - hash of previous 1 or 2 tokens, green fraction 0.5
EXP-edit (Kuditipudi et al. 2023) - distortion-free sampling watermark
SWEET (Lee et al. 2023) and SIR (Liu et al. 2023b) - non-agnostic baselines, appendix only
